name: Test-Driven Development (RED-GREEN-REFACTOR)
description: Write failing tests first, implement minimal passing code, then refactor. Never skip the RED phase.
source: Kent Beck: Test-Driven Development by Example

name: Systematic Debugging & Root Cause Analysis
description: Follow a structured debugging methodology: reproduce, isolate, hypothesize, verify, fix. Never guess.
source: The Pragmatic Programmer: Root Cause Analysis
template:
## Systematic Debugging

### The 5-Step RCA Process
1. **Reproduce** — Create a minimal, reliable repro case. If you can't reproduce it, you can't fix it.
2. **Isolate** — Binary search the codebase. Comment out halves until the bug disappears.
3. **Hypothesize** — Form a specific hypothesis: "The bug is caused by X because Y."
4. **Verify** — Test the hypothesis. Add logging/breakpoints to confirm the root cause.
5. **Fix** — Fix the *root cause*, not the symptom. Verify the fix doesn't break other tests.

### Rules
- Never fix a bug you don't understand
- Fix root causes, not symptoms
- Add a regression test for every bug fixed
- Document the cause in the commit message

### Anti-patterns to avoid
- Guess-and-check without a hypothesis
- Fixing symptoms (e.g., catching and swallowing errors)
- Not adding a regression test
- Blaming frameworks before verifying your own code

### Checklist
- [ ] Minimal repro case created
- [ ] Root cause identified (not just symptom)
- [ ] Fix addresses root cause
- [ ] Regression test added
- [ ] No unrelated changes in the fix

name: Get Shit Done — Pragmatic Delivery
description: Prioritize shipping working software. Avoid perfectionism paralysis. Deliver value incrementally.
source: Agile Manifesto: Working software over comprehensive documentation
template:
## Get Shit Done (GSD)

### Core Principle
Working software > perfect software. Ship an 80% solution today rather than a 100% solution never.

### GSD Rules
1. **Time-box decisions** — If you've spent > 15 min deciding between two approaches, pick one and move.
2. **Vertical slices** — Ship end-to-end thin slices (feature works, even if ugly) before full polish.
3. **Defer non-blocking work** — Mark TODOs but don't block shipping on them.
4. **Delete over refactor** — If code blocks progress, delete and rewrite faster than untangling.
5. **Favour built-in over imported** — Don't add a dependency for < 10 lines of code.

### When NOT to apply
- Security-critical code (no shortcuts)
- Database migrations (no shortcuts)
- Public API contracts (breaking changes need care)

### Checklist
- [ ] Core user flow working end-to-end
- [ ] Blocking issues resolved (non-blocking deferred)
- [ ] No security shortcuts taken
- [ ] TODOs filed for deferred work

name: Subagent-Driven Development
description: Delegate specialized tasks to subagents (code review, testing, security analysis) rather than doing everything in one agent context.
source: Claude Code Agent SDK: Multi-agent patterns
template:
## Subagent-Driven Development

### Core Pattern
Orchestrate specialized subagents for distinct tasks. Don't do everything in one context window.

### Agent Delegation Matrix
| Task | Delegate To |
|------|-------------|
| Code review | code-reviewer agent |
| Security audit | security-reviewer agent |
| E2E tests | e2e-runner agent |
| Build errors | build-error-resolver agent |
| Architecture decisions | architect agent |
| Dead code cleanup | refactor-cleaner agent |

### Rules
1. Delegate when task is independently executable (no shared mutable state)
2. Pass complete context in the prompt — subagents have no conversation memory
3. Use background agents for non-blocking work (reviews, tests)
4. Validate agent output before acting on it
5. Don't delegate decisions that require the full conversation context

### Checklist
- [ ] Task is independently executable
- [ ] Subagent prompt is self-contained with full context
- [ ] Output validated before acting
- [ ] No circular dependency between agents

name: Dispatching Parallel Agents
description: Run independent subagents in parallel to maximize throughput. Never sequence what can run concurrently.
source: Claude Code Agent SDK: Concurrent subagent workflows
template:
## Dispatching Parallel Agents

### Core Rule
If tasks are independent (no shared output dependencies), run them in parallel via a single message with multiple tool calls.

### Parallelization Decision Tree
```
Task A depends on Task B output?
  YES → Sequential
  NO  → Parallel
```

### Good Parallel Candidates
- Code review + security review + type check (all read-only)
- Multiple file explorations
- Testing different modules simultaneously
- Documentation + linting (independent)

### Bad Parallel Candidates
- Write file A, then read file A (sequential)
- Build, then deploy (sequential)
- Generate plan, then execute plan (sequential)

### Example dispatch
```
// Single message, 3 parallel Agent calls:
Agent 1: Security analysis of auth.js
Agent 2: Performance review of cache.js  
Agent 3: Type check of utils/
```

### Checklist
- [ ] Tasks confirmed independent (no output→input chain)
- [ ] All agents dispatched in single message
- [ ] Each agent prompt is self-contained
- [ ] Results collected before proceeding

name: Enforcement Pattern — Rules as Code
description: Encode standards as automated checks that run on every change. Rules not enforced by tooling are not rules.
source: Google Engineering Practices: Automation over convention
template:
## Enforcement Pattern

### Principle
If a rule matters, automate its enforcement. Convention documents are ignored; CI gates are not.

### Enforcement Layers (strongest to weakest)
1. **Type system** — TypeScript strict mode catches category of errors at compile time
2. **Linter rules** — ESLint/Stylelint/Biome block bad patterns before commit
3. **Pre-commit hooks** — Husky + lint-staged run on staged files
4. **CI gates** — GitHub Actions fail the PR on violations
5. **Code review** — Human reviewer catches what tools miss
6. **Documentation** — Weakest; ignored under pressure

### Implementation
```json
// .eslintrc: enforce at lint time
{ "rules": { "no-console": "error", "@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any": "error" } }

// .husky/pre-commit: enforce at commit
"npx lint-staged"

// .github/workflows/ci.yml: enforce at PR
"run: npm run lint && npm test"
```

### Checklist
- [ ] Critical rules enforced by linter (not just documented)
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks prevent bad commits
- [ ] CI gate fails PR on violations
- [ ] New rules added to automation, not just wiki

name: Four-Phase Development Loop
description: Research → Plan → Implement → Verify. Never skip phases. Revisit earlier phases when blocked.
template:
## Four-Phase Development Loop

### Phase 1: Research
- Understand the problem domain before writing code
- Search for existing implementations (GitHub, npm, docs)
- Identify constraints (perf budget, API limits, compatibility)
- Output: clear problem statement + known constraints

### Phase 2: Plan
- Design the solution architecture
- Identify files to create/modify
- Write pseudocode or interface definitions first
- Estimate scope; flag unknowns
- Output: implementation plan with file list

### Phase 3: Implement
- Follow the plan; update plan when it changes
- Write tests alongside or before code (TDD preferred)
- Commit frequently (logical units, not time-based)
- Output: working implementation with tests

### Phase 4: Verify
- Run all tests; confirm they pass
- Manually test critical paths
- Code review (self or peer)
- Output: verified, reviewed, deployable code

### Rules
- Never skip straight to Phase 3
- When blocked in Phase 3, return to Phase 2
- Phase 4 is mandatory — "it compiles" is not verification

### Checklist
- [ ] Research phase completed (problem understood)
- [ ] Plan created (files identified, approach clear)
- [ ] Implementation follows plan
- [ ] Verification completed (tests pass, manual check done)

name: Spec-Driven Dev Loop + Context Management
description: Write a spec before code. Use the spec to manage AI context window efficiently. Re-inject spec when context degrades.
template:
## Spec-Driven Dev Loop

### Core Concept
A spec is both a plan and a context anchor. It keeps long AI sessions on track and prevents context window drift.

### Spec Format
```markdown
## Goal
One sentence: what this change achieves.

## Constraints
- Must not break X
- Must work with Y
- Performance budget: Z

## Approach
Step-by-step plan with file names.

## Done When
- [ ] Test A passes
- [ ] Behaviour B works in browser
- [ ] No regressions in C
```

### Context Management Rules
1. Write spec at session start; save to plan file
2. When context gets long (> 50% used), re-inject spec summary
3. After each major step, update the Done When checklist
4. If AI drifts, re-anchor with: "Refer to spec: [paste Done When section]"
5. New session = new spec injection

### Checklist
- [ ] Spec written before first code change
- [ ] Done When criteria defined and testable
- [ ] Spec updated as plan evolves
- [ ] Context re-anchored when window >50% full

name: Two-Stage Code Review
description: Stage 1: automated checks (lint, types, tests). Stage 2: human/AI semantic review. Never merge on Stage 1 alone.
source: Google Code Review Guidelines: Two-pass review process
template:
## Two-Stage Code Review

### Stage 1: Automated (must pass before Stage 2)
- Lint: zero errors, zero warnings
- Type check: zero TypeScript errors
- Unit tests: all pass, coverage >= 80%
- Security scan: no CRITICAL/HIGH findings
- Bundle size: within budget

### Stage 2: Semantic (human or AI reviewer)
Focus on what automation can't catch:
- **Correctness**: Does the code do what the PR description says?
- **Edge cases**: Missing error handling, boundary conditions?
- **Design**: Is the abstraction right? Too complex? Too simple?
- **Security**: Business logic flaws that tools miss?
- **Maintainability**: Will future devs understand this in 6 months?

### Stage 2 Checklist
- [ ] Code does what PR description claims
- [ ] Error paths handled (network fail, empty state, null)
- [ ] No hardcoded values (use constants/config)
- [ ] No commented-out code
- [ ] New functionality has tests
- [ ] Breaking changes documented

### Never Merge If
- Stage 1 is failing
- Reviewer has CRITICAL/BLOCK comment unresolved
- PR description doesn't explain the *why*

name: Concurrent Subagent Workflows
description: Design agent workflows where independent tasks execute concurrently using fan-out/fan-in patterns.
source: Claude Code Agent SDK: Concurrent execution patterns
template:
## Concurrent Subagent Workflows

### Fan-Out / Fan-In Pattern
```
Orchestrator
  ├─ Agent A (parallel)
  ├─ Agent B (parallel)
  └─ Agent C (parallel)
        ↓ (all complete)
  Aggregator → synthesize results
```

### Implementation Rules
1. **Fan-out**: Dispatch all independent agents in a single message (multiple tool calls)
2. **Agent isolation**: Each agent gets full self-contained context; no shared state
3. **Fan-in**: Wait for all agents before synthesizing (don't act on partial results)
4. **Error handling**: One failing agent should not block others; handle individually
5. **Result validation**: Verify agent output before using it in next step

### Common Patterns
- **Parallel research**: 3 agents each explore a different part of the codebase
- **Parallel review**: Code review + security review + performance review simultaneously
- **Parallel generation**: Generate tests + implementation + docs in parallel

### Checklist
- [ ] Independent tasks identified (no data dependencies)
- [ ] All fan-out agents dispatched in single message
- [ ] Each agent prompt self-contained
- [ ] Fan-in aggregates all results before proceeding
- [ ] Failed agents handled gracefully

name: Writing Plans — Structured Implementation Plans
description: Write a concrete implementation plan with file paths, function signatures, and build order before coding.
template:
## Writing Implementation Plans

### Plan Structure
```markdown
## Context
Why this change exists. What problem it solves.

## Approach
High-level design decision + rationale.

## Files to Change
| File | Action | Why |
|------|--------|-----|
| src/foo.ts | modify | add new method |
| src/bar.ts | create | new module |

## Build Order
1. Create types/interfaces first (no deps)
2. Create utilities (depend only on types)
3. Create main logic (depends on utilities)
4. Update entry points last

## Verification
- Run: npm test
- Manual: [describe what to click/call]
- Regression: existing tests still pass
```

### Plan Quality Rules
- Every file mentioned must have a specific reason
- Build order must respect dependencies (no circular)
- Verification must be executable (not vague)
- Breaking changes must be called out explicitly

### Checklist
- [ ] Context explains why (not just what)
- [ ] Files list with specific reasons
- [ ] Build order respects dependencies
- [ ] Verification is concrete and runnable

name: Executing Plans — Plan-to-Code Pipeline
description: Execute implementation plans systematically: mark tasks as you go, update plan when it changes, verify at each milestone.
template:
## Executing Implementation Plans

### Execution Rules
1. **Read plan before starting** — Don't code from memory of the plan
2. **One task at a time** — Mark in-progress before starting, completed before moving on
3. **Update plan when reality diverges** — The plan is a living document
4. **Verify after each milestone** — Don't batch verification to the end
5. **Surface blockers early** — Flag blocked tasks immediately, don't work around silently

### Execution Checklist Pattern
For each task in the plan:
- [ ] Task marked in_progress
- [ ] Implementation complete
- [ ] Tests pass for this task
- [ ] Task marked completed
- [ ] Plan updated if approach changed

### Milestone Verification
After every 3-5 tasks:
```bash
npm test           # tests still pass
npm run lint       # no new lint errors
git diff --stat    # changes match the plan
```

### When Plan Changes
- Update the plan file immediately
- Note why it changed (new constraint discovered, better approach found)
- Don't silently deviate; keep plan and code in sync

### Checklist
- [ ] Plan read before execution starts
- [ ] Tasks tracked (not batched)
- [ ] Milestone verification done mid-execution
- [ ] Plan updated for any deviations
